segments.s745-g225: دل زاده فرخ is a noun phrase in which دل‌زاده ('one born of the heart' / beloved) and فرخ (an epithet meaning 'fortunate' here) form a compounded descriptive subject. نگه داشت is a past-tense compound verb (نگه‌داشتن) meaning 'kept/held his look' or 'kept gazing'; داشت here functions as the past auxiliary within the verbal unit. نیز placed after the verb is a linking adverb ('also/likewise') tying this action to the following clause.
segments.s745-g226: سپه را همه is the definite object ('the whole army') with را as the object marker and همه emphasizing 'entire/whole'; Persian allows the object to appear before the verb for emphasis. روی برگاشت is a verbal phrase where روی ('face') plus برگاشت (past of برداشتن/برگاشتن in poetic use) means 'he turned/lifted his face toward' — a common poetic way to say 'turned his face'. The final نیز again functions as 'also/likewise', linking this movement to the prior gaze.
segments.s745-g41: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic). آگاه شد is a past-tense verbal phrase 'became aware' (آگاه + شد), where شد is the past auxiliary. زان سخن is the object/phrase 'of that speech' (زان = 'of that'), and شهریار (the subject) appears at the end by poetic inversion: literal order 'When became-aware of that speech the king.'
segments.s745-g42: همی‌داشت is the classical continuous/past imperfective sense ('he held/considered' — همی marks ongoing aspect with a past stem). آن کار دشوار is a noun phrase 'that difficult affair' (آن = demonstrative, کار = affair, دشوار = difficult). خوار is a predicative adjective meaning 'contemptible/despised' applied to that affair; word order places the judgement after the noun for emphasis.
segments.s745-g43: بدانست is a past verb 'he knew/realized' (بـ- prefixed form of دانست). کان هست is an archaic copular phrase meaning 'that is' / 'this is' (کان as an old demonstrative/introductory particle + هست). کار گراز = 'the boar's work/doing' (کار = affair; گراز = the boar), so the clause states he perceived that the affair was the boar's doing.
segments.s745-g44: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/that'. گفته ست is a reported/perfect sense 'it is said/has been said' (گفته + ست, where ست = 'is' in an archaic/perfect-reporting use). با قیصر رزمساز is a prepositional phrase 'in league/with Caesar, the rouse-maker of war' (رزمساز is an epithet). The whole clause reports that this had been said to be done with/alongside Caesar.
segments.s745-g45: بدان کش here functions as a compact/poetic expression meaning 'to that (chorus/party)' or 'those people' (بـ- as prefix + دان/کش forming a bound phrase); in context it introduces the action that follows. همی‌خواند is the continuous past 'kept singing/chanting' (همی + خواند). و او چاره‌جست = 'and he sought a remedy' (چاره‌جست is past tense 'sought a solution'). The clause links a continuing chant with the king's attempt to find a way out.
segments.s745-g46: همی‌داشت again denotes a continuing/past sense 'was holding/was in the state of.' آن نامور شاه is 'that renowned king' (آن = demonstrative; نامور = famed; شاه = king). سست is the predicative adjective 'weak/unstable,' so the clause states that the famed king remained weak or indecisive.
segments.s745-g47: ز پرویز is a prepositional phrase 'of/from Parviz' (ز = 'from/of' in poetic register). ترسان functions as a predicative adjective 'frightened' (they were frightened of Parviz). بد آن بدنشان literally 'bad that their bodies' = 'their condition was bad' (بد = ill/bad, آن بدنشان = that their persons/bodies), so the clause says their state was ill because they feared Parviz.
segments.s745-g48: ز درگاه او is 'from his court/presence' (درگاه = royal court/presence, او = his). هم = 'also/even'. ز گردنکشان = 'from the rebels/proud ones' (ز = from; گردنکشان here denotes rebellious/proud men). The parallel 'ز ... هم ز ...' emphasizes that their bad condition applied both in his court and among the rebellious/proud.
segments.s745-g1: بدان is an older fused prepositional phrase (ب + آن) meaning “to/at that” and introduces the location; the sequence نامور تخت و جای مهی is a noun phrase in which two coordinate nouns (تخت “throne” and جای “seat/place”) are modified by adjectives (نامور “renowned” before تخت, مهی “moon‑like/beautiful” after جای). Word order is typical literary Persian: locative preposition + descriptive noun phrase.
segments.s745-g2: بزرگی و دیهیم شاهنشهی is a coordinated nominal phrase giving two attributes of kingship; بزرگی ‘greatness/grandeur’ is a noun in the abstract, و links the two nouns, دیهیم is an archaic/obscure adjective or epithet qualifying شاهنشهی (the second noun, ‘kingship/imperial rule’). The whole phrase apposites the locative phrase before it (i.e. what is on that throne).
segments.s745-g3: جهاندار is a compound title meaning ‘world‑ruler/sovereign’ and functions as the subject; هم here is an adverbial particle meaning ‘too/also’ (literary position before the predicate), داستانی (‘a tale/story’) is the object/noun, and نکرد is the past negative of کردن “to do” meaning ‘did not do’ — together ‘also did not tell a tale’ (i.e. did not make up a story).
segments.s745-g4: از ایران و توران sets a source/location (‘from Iran and Turan’); برآورد گرد is a literary verbal cluster in which برآورد is a causative/bringing verb and گرد is an older verbal auxiliary (here indicating the action was carried out or resulted). The whole clause means that things or people were brought/raised from Iran and Turan (the exact object is implicit).
segments.s745-g5: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning ‘when’; آن is the demonstrative ‘that’ pointing back to the king just mentioned; دادگر شاه is a noun phrase ‘the just king’ (دادگر modifying شاه), and بیداد گشت is a predicative phrase where بیداد ‘injustice/oppression’ is used with گشت (past of گشتن ‘to become/turn’) to mean ‘became unjust/turned to oppression’. The clause describes a change of the king’s character.
segments.s745-g6: ز بیدادی introduces the cause/means (‘from/because of that injustice’); the following کهتران is a problematic or dialectal form in the data (it functions here as a plural referring to ‘the lesser/lowly ones’ or similar), and شادگشت is a past tense verb meaning ‘rejoiced/was glad’. Grammatically the clause says that as a result of the king’s injustice the lower people rejoiced.
segments.s745-g7: بیامد is the simple past verb ‘came/arrived’; it precedes the proper name فرخ‌زاد آزرمگان (a two‑part name or name plus epithet) which is the subject — a common literary order in Persian where the verb may come before the noun for emphasis. The whole is a straightforward past‑tense clause: ‘Farrokhzad Azarmagan came.’
segments.s745-g8: دژم روی is an adjectival phrase meaning ‘grim‑faced’ (دژم ‘sullen/grim’ modifying روی ‘face’); با is the preposition ‘with’, followed by زیردستان ‘subordinates/retainers’ as the noun, and ژکان appears to be a tribal or group name (or a dialectal plural) identifying those retainers. The phrase as a whole describes his arrival accompanied by his followers.
segments.s745-g17: هر آنکس ... introduces a generalizing relative: هر (every/whenever) + آنکس (that person/who) = 'whoever'. کزان is a contracted compound of 'ک + ز + آن' meaning 'from which / than that' (poetic order), and بتری is the comparative 'worse'. یافت is the past/optative stem 'found/obtained' used here in a generalizing past sense ('encountered'/'met with'). بهر is a preposition 'for/with respect to' linking the comparative phrase to the following clause.
segments.s745-g18: همی before the verb is a poetic/progressive marker meaning 'always/constantly' (or 'would'); دود نفرین is a noun phrase 'smoke of curse' (dud = smoke, نفرین = curse). برآمد is the past/derived perfective 'arose/issued forth'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and شهر = 'city'. Word order is subjectless: the smoke 'arose from the city' as the result of the preceding conditional/generalizing clause.
segments.s745-g19: یکی introduces an indefinite 'a certain / there was one'. بی‌هنر is a compound adjective 'without skill' = 'unskilled'. بود is the past copula 'was'. نامش is 'his name' (نام + enclitic ش = his) and گراز is the name/nickname 'Goraz' (literally 'boar' used as a name or epithet). The phrase is a simple nominal sentence: 'There was a certain unskilled man; his name was Goraz.'
segments.s745-g20: کزو = 'from whom' (ک + ز + او/آن) links back to گراز. یافتی is the past form of یافتن 'to find/obtain' used in a generalizing sense 'one would get / one found'. خواب و آرام و ناز is a triad of nouns: 'sleep, repose/peace, and pampering/indulgence'—objects of یافتی. Word order is typical: relative ('from whom') + verb + list of objects.
segments.s745-g21: که introduces a relative clause 'who'. بودی is the past copula 'was' (archaic/poetic ending -ی instead of بود). همیشه 'always' modifies نگهبان 'guardian'. روم here is the proper name 'Rum' (Byzantium/land of the Romans). The clause literally reads 'who was always guardian of Rum.'
segments.s745-g22: یکی at the start functions existentially 'there was one'. دیو سر is a compound where سر = 'chief/head' gives 'chief demon' (word order noun + modifier). بود is the past copula 'was'. بیداد and شوم are predicates: بیداد = 'oppression/injustice' (here describing cruelty) and شوم = adjective 'ill‑omened/evil'. The whole sentence: 'There was a demon‑chief, oppressive and ill‑omened.'
segments.s745-g23: چو = 'when'. شد is the perfective/becoming verb 'became'. شاه = 'the king'. با داد here literally 'with justice' (an adverbial phrase) and بیدادگر is an adjectival/epithet 'tyrant/oppressor'—poetically the phrase says the king 'became, with (a show of) justice, an oppressor' (or 'when the king, justly or by right, became an oppressor'). Word order is compressed, typical of epic diction.
segments.s745-g24: از ایران = 'from Iran' (ز/از + place). نخست = 'first' (adverb). او is the pronoun 'he'. بپیچید is the past form of پیچیدن with the prefix بـ (here literary) meaning 'turned' or 'averted' (بـ + پیچید = 'he turned'). سر = 'head' used idiomatically for 'turned his head' = 'looked/turned away'. The clause means 'he first turned his head away from Iran.'
segments.s745-g201: کزین سان is a poetic compound meaning 'of/from such' or 'of this sort' (کزین = 'from this', سان = 'such/like') and introduces the noun phrase that follows. سپاهی is the noun 'host/army' with the adjectives دلیر و جوان after it (noun + adjectives), so the phrase reads 'a host/army brave and young'.
segments.s745-g202: نبینم is the 1st-person present negative 'I do not see' (classical usage); کس here means 'anyone' and functions as the (indefinite) object of نبینم. اندر میان = 'in/within the midst' (اندر = 'in/within', میان = 'middle/midst'). ناتوان is an adjective modifying the implied people: 'the powerless'.
segments.s745-g203: شما را is a topical/object-marked 'you' (را as object marker). چرا بیم باشد ز شاه literally asks 'why should there be fear of the king'—بیم is the noun 'fear' and باشد is the classical present/subjunctive copula meaning 'should be / be'. ز introduces the source ('of/from the king').
segments.s745-g204: به گیتی 'in/throughout the world' uses به as a locative/adverbial marker with گیتی 'world'. پراگنده is an adjective 'scattered/widespread' modifying سپاه, and دارد is the present 'has/possesses' (dative construction: 'he has an army [that is] scattered in the world').
segments.s745-g205: بزرگی is the noun 'greatness' and is the object of نبینم 'I do not see'. به درگاه اوی is a locative phrase 'in/at his court/presence' (به = 'at/in', درگاه = 'royal court/presence') with اوی the archaic enclitic form meaning 'his'.
segments.s745-g206: که introduces a relative clause modifying بزرگی ('that/which'). روشن کند is a causative construction: روشن (lit. 'bright') with کند 'makes/does', so 'that would make bright / illuminate'. اختر و ماه اوی = 'his star and moon' (اوی = 'his'), the things said to be illuminated by that greatness.
segments.s745-g207: شما is 'you' and خوار دارید is a light-verbal expression meaning 'you deem [something] low/despised' (خوار 'despised', دارید 'you have/hold'). گفتار من 'my speech' is the object placed after the verb phrase in this poetic order: 'you hold my speech cheap'.
segments.s745-g208: مترسید here is the negative imperative/formal hortative 'do not fear'. یک سر is an idiomatic quantifier (literally 'one head') meaning 'even a bit / even for an instant' and modifies the following prepositional phrase ز آزار من 'from my harm' (ز = 'from', آزار من = 'my harm'). The whole clause means 'do not fear even a bit of harm from me.'
segments.s745-g169: کس here functions as an indefinite negative 'no one' (subject placed before the object). آن راز is the direct object 'that secret'. پیدا is used in the sense 'found/discovered' and نیارست is a poetic/archaic negative/past-form meaning 'did not manage/was not able'; کرد is a light past auxiliary ('did') that completes the verbal phrase. Word order follows classical Persian (subject — object — verbal complex).
segments.s745-g170: بماندند is past plural 'they remained' (or 'they were left'). با introduces accompaniment/condition 'with' and درد here means emotional sorrow/grief. The conjunction و links to رخساره زرد, where رخساره (noun) is followed by the adjective زرد ('pale') — Persian places adjectives after nouns. The whole phrase describes their lingering sorrow and pale complexions.
segments.s745-g171: پیمبر ('messenger') is the subject; یکی 'a certain' introduces indefiniteness. بد به دل (literally 'bad to/for heart') is the idiom 'to have ill will in the heart' (بد modifies the inner disposition, and به + دل marks the location 'in the heart'). با گراز uses با to mark accompaniment or a simile ('with a boar' = like a boar), i.e. a figurative comparison.
segments.s745-g172: همی‌داشت is the imperfective/past habitual poetic form (همی- + داشت) meaning 'he had/kept' or 'was holding'. از introduces the source/what the secret was kept from; آب و (here written وز) باد 'water and wind' is a poetic pairing meaning 'from the elements' — وز is an archaic/poetic connective here. راز is the object 'secret'. The phrase means he kept the secret hidden even from water and wind.
segments.s745-g173: بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. نهانی is an adverb 'secretly/covertly' and follows the verb (common in classical Persian). به نزدیکشان is a prepositional locative phrase 'to their vicinity/near them', with the possessive enclitic -شان 'their' attached.
segments.s745-g174: برافروخت is a transitive past verb 'he kindled/ignited' (figuratively: inflamed). جانهای is the plural object 'souls/lives' and تاریکشان is the adjective 'dark' with the enclitic -شان ('their') attached to it; Persian places the adjective after the noun, and possession is expressed by the enclitic on the adjective here.
segments.s745-g175: مترسید here is an archaic negative imperative meaning 'do not fear' (addressing the audience). The sequence مترسید گفت should be read as a quoted imperative followed by the reporting verb 'گفت' ('he said'). ای بزرگان is the direct address 'O great ones/elders' and که شاه begins the subordinate clause explaining why they should not fear.
segments.s745-g176: ندید is the negative past 'did not see' (the subject 'شاه' carries over from the previous segment). از شما marks the domain/source 'in you' or 'of you', آشکارا is an adverb 'openly/clearly' modifying the noun that follows, and گناه is the object 'sin/fault'. Together: 'the king did not see any open/clear sin in you.'
segments.s745-g161: چنین بود + noun phrase is a poetic word order meaning 'thus was (the) message' — چنين (thus) precedes بود (past of بودن) and the message follows; نزد سپاه = 'with/to the army' or 'before the army' (نزد = in the presence/of the group).
segments.s745-g162: که introduces the content clause ('that ...'); از پیش بودی = 'you had been beforehand/previously' (بودِی is past tense 'were'); مرا + را is the object 'me' with the object marker را (here split as م + را in the segmentation), and نیک‌خواه is a compound noun 'well-wisher/a friend who wishes well.'
segments.s745-g163: چرا راه دادی is an idiomatic question 'why did you give the way/allow (passage)?' — راه here means 'way' (in the sense of allowing movement) and دادی is past 'you gave/allowed'; که begins the clause describing what was allowed (قیصر ز روم = 'Caesar from Rome').
segments.s745-g164: بیاورد is a present-subjunctive/form used in reported or purposive contexts here meaning 'to bring' (in English rendered past: 'to bring' / 'could bring'); لشکر is the direct object 'an army'; بدین (written here as ب + دین) + مرز و بوم = 'to/into this border and land' — ب is the 'to/into' element and مرز و بوم = 'frontier and homeland.'
segments.s745-g165: بود آنک = 'it was he who...' (آنک is a demonstrative/relative 'that one'); از راه یزدان = 'from the way of God' (یزدان = God), and بگشت here is an archaic/past-form meaning 'turned away' or 'strayed' (an older variant of برگشت in this context).
segments.s745-g166: ز راه و ز پیمان ما = 'from the way and from our covenant' with ز repeated for emphasis; برگذشت (past) here means 'passed beyond/abandoned' or 'violated' (i.e., he did not keep the covenant).
segments.s745-g167: چو = 'when' (temporal), پیغام خسرو = 'Khosrow's message' (genitive), شنید is past 'heard'; the demonstrative آن before سپاه ('that army') follows the verb in inverted poetic order: 'when the army heard the message of Khosrow.'
segments.s745-g168: شد + predicate: شد = 'became' and سیاه = 'black/dark' (here 'faces grew dark'); از بیم رخسار ایشان = 'from fear, their countenances' — the phrase is inverted for poetic effect: literally 'became, from fear, their countenances dark.' ایشان refers back to the army.
segments.s745-g9: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from/of' and introduces the relativized noun phrase 'هرکس خواسته' ('who had asked/wished'). همی (hami) is the frequentive particle used with past verbs in epic Persian to indicate repeated or habitual action. خواسته is a past participle meaning 'had asked/wished', and بستدی (bastedi) is an older third-person past form 'he bound/closed'; together they mean ‘he bound whoever had asked’ with the implied subject (he) omitted as is common in Persian.
segments.s745-g10: همی (hami) again marks repeated action. This phrase lists targets with demonstratives (این 'this' and آن 'that') and prepositional/locative elements (بران, بر, بر) around the verb زدی (zadi, 'struck'). The sequence is idiomatic: subject omitted, then 'this (one) here and that (one) there' followed by the past verb, so the whole reads 'he would/kept striking this and that.' Note that some tokens (بران, بر, ین) are poetic or orthographic forms marking location/attachment rather than changing the basic meaning.
segments.s745-g11: به ... شد is a common verbal construction meaning 'became into/turned into.' Here به takes the sense 'into/towards' and نفرین (nafrin) is 'curse'; شد is the past 'became.' آن آفرینهای پیش is a noun phrase 'those acclamations of before/earlier' (آن 'that/those', آفرینهای 'acclamations/praises' in plural/construct, پیش 'before/previous'). The whole segment reads 'those earlier acclamations became a curse.'
segments.s745-g12: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/so that/when'). چون here is a simile particle meaning 'like/as', followed by the simile's pattern: گرگ (wolf) modified by the adjective بیدادگر ('tyrannical, oppressive') and the past intransitive گشت ('became'). The subject میش (mīsh, 'ewe') follows the verb, a common poetic order; the clause literal reading is 'that, like a wolf, the ewe became tyrannical.'
segments.s745-g13: بیاراست (bi-ārāst) is a past/preterite verb 'adorned/arranged' and here is used figuratively as 'he put/laid upon himself.' بر (bar) is the preposition 'on/upon', خویشتن (khvīshtan) is the emphatic 'himself', and رنج نو is the noun phrase 'new suffering' (رنج 'suffering', نو 'new'). Word order has the verb first (poetic) and the object/prepositional phrase after it.
segments.s745-g14: نکرد (nakard) is the past negative of کردن ('did not do'), used here with آرزو (ārūzū, 'wish/desire') to mean 'he did not desire' or 'he made no wish.' جز (juz) means 'except', and همه گنج نو is the noun phrase 'all (the) new treasure(s)' (همه 'all', گنج 'treasure', نو 'new'). The clause thus states 'he desired nothing except all new treasure.' Note that classical Persian often places the verb before its object and uses نکرد to negate the action.
segments.s745-g15: چو (chu) is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The coordinated predicates بی‌آب و بی‌نان و بی تن are negative-descriptive compounds meaning 'without water, without bread, and without body/person' (بی‑ as the prefix 'without' attached to آب 'water', نان 'bread', تن 'body'). شدند (shodand) is the third-person plural past 'they became/were.' The whole clause is temporal: 'When they became without water, bread, and body...' (i.e., destitute).
segments.s745-g16: ز ایران سوی شهر is a locative/directional phrase: ز (z) 'from', ایران 'Iran', سوی 'toward(s)', شهر 'the city.' دشمن شدند (doshman shodand) is 'they became enemies' or 'they became hostile.' The construction places the source and direction before the predicate 'became enemies', so 'they became enemies, coming from Iran toward the city' or more smoothly 'they turned into enemies as they moved from Iran toward the city.'
segments.s745-g25: This noun phrase names a person: دگر زاد فرخ literally 'another birth/offspring, Farrokh'. دگر (preceding noun) marks 'another' or 'a different one'; زاد is a noun 'birth/offspring' (not a verb here) and immediately follows it as the head; فرخ is the personal name. The relative particle که begins a subordinate clause 'who/whose…' and is followed by نامی بدی 'having a bad name' (نامی = 'famous/noted', بدی = 'evil/bad' describing the reputation).
segments.s745-g26: This is a prepositional phrase of location plus a (poetic/archaic) copula. به نزدیک = 'near/at the approach of'; خسرو گرامی = 'the honored Khosrow' (noun followed by adjectival epithet). The final word بدی here is not the noun 'evil' but an archaic/poetic form (cf. بودی) functioning as a past copula 'was' — i.e. 'was near'.
segments.s745-g27: نیارست is an archaic/poetic negative/impersonal verb meaning roughly 'it was not fitting/possible' or 'could not/was not permitted', so the clause reads 'نیارست کس رفت نزدیک شاه' = 'no one (کس) could go (رفت) near (نزدیک) the king (شاه)'. را is not present here; the structure is impersonal negative + noun + past verb.
segments.s745-g28: This clause names the actors and their desire: همه = 'all/everyone'; زاد فرخ = 'the offspring/line of Farrokh' (زاد as noun 'birth/offspring' followed by the name); بدی بار = 'the burden of evil' where بدی is the noun 'evil' and بار = 'burden/responsibility' (i.e. what one bears); خواه is the present/past-root of 'to want' used here to mean 'desired/sought'. Together: 'All of Farrokh's offspring desired to bear evil.'
segments.s745-g29: شهنشاه را is the marked topic/object phrase 'the emperor' (را as topical/object marker). چون پرآمد = temporal 'when/when it rose' or 'when...became full' (چون here introducing a temporal clause). پرآمد is a compound past meaning 'became full/rose up'. قفیز is an obscure/rare noun (here the subject of پرآمد) whose exact sense is uncertain in classical usage; grammatically it is the thing that 'became full' in the shah.
segments.s745-g30: دل زاد فرخ = 'the heart of Farrokh's offspring' (دل 'heart' followed by the genitive phrase zād Farrokh). تبه گشت = 'became wicked/ruined' where تبه is an adjective/noun 'wicked/ruinous' and گشت is the past of گشتن 'became'. نیز = 'also/likewise', linking this change to the previous clause.
segments.s745-g31: یکی گشت = 'one (a certain person) went/turned' (یکی as an indefinite 'a certain one'); گشت here is the past of گشتن with the sense 'went/roamed'. با سالخورده گراز = 'with the aged G̲r̲āz' (با = 'with'; سالخورده = 'old/aged' modifying گراز, which here functions as a personal nickname or epithet 'the boar'). The whole segment describes a single agent going accompanied by the old man nicknamed 'G̲r̲āz'.
segments.s745-g32: ز کشور به کشور = a fixed idiom 'from country to country' (ز = 'from' poetic; کشور repeated to indicate movement through realms). به پیوست راز = 'the secret was joined/carried along' where پیوست (here verbal) means 'attached/was carried on' and راز = 'secret'. Grammatically this states the motion/propagation of the secret from realm to realm.
segments.s745-g33: گراز سپهبد is a name + title (Goraz, the spahbed) acting as the subject. یکی is an indefinite determiner 'a certain' (here introducing the action). نامه کرد is a common compound where نامه (letter) is the object and کرد (past of کردن) is the light verb meaning 'wrote/sent' the letter.
segments.s745-g34: به قیصر is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the Caesar'. The clause then continues with و (and) + را (object marker) + نیز 'also' before the predicative بدکامه کرد: here بدکامه is a predicative adjective 'ill‑disposed/malicious' and کرد is the light verb 'made/caused (him to be)'; together 'he also made the Caesar ill‑disposed.' Note the object marker را refers back to the person (the Caesar).
segments.s745-g35: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو is the indirect object 'to him' + گفت 'said'). برخیز و ایران بگیر are two imperatives (برخیز 'rise/stand up', بگیر 'take/seize') placed in sequence with و 'and' connecting them; ایران is the direct object of بگیر.
segments.s745-g36: نخستین 'first' modifies the following clause. من آیم uses آیم (present/subjunctive form of آمدن) with a future/potential sense 'I will come' or 'I shall be the first to come.' تو را دستگیر is a purposive or result phrase where تو را (you + object marker) and دستگیر (to seize/arrest) indicate the intended action: 'I will come to arrest you.'
segments.s745-g37: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. آن نامه برخواند is a verb phrase 'read that letter aloud' (برخواند = read/recited). قیصر follows as the subject/topic (inverted order) and سپاه is the following noun 'the army' that will be governed by the main verb in the next segment.
segments.s745-g38: فراز آورید is a verbal phrase 'brought up / raised' (فراز = 'up/on high', آورید = past/plural form 'brought' or a command in older usage); از در رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the gate/entrance of the battlefield' (در here = gate/door). Together: 'he brought up the army from the battlefield gate.'
segments.s745-g39: بیاورد لشکر is verb + object 'he brought/caused the army (to come)'. هم آنگه 'also/then' is a connective adverbial ('likewise, then'). ز روم is a prepositional phrase 'from Rome' (here meaning Byzantium/the Romans) specifying origin.
segments.s745-g40: بیامد is a past verb 'came'. سوی 'toward' introduces the destination, مرز آباد بوم is a compound noun phrase 'the border of the prosperous/settled homeland' (مرز = border, آباد = prosperous/inhabited, بوم = land/homeland). The phrase describes movement toward the fertile/settled frontier.
segments.s745-g177: مباشید is a negative imperative (2nd-person plural/formal) meaning 'do not be'. جز here is the exception marker 'except'. The phrase یک دل و یک‌زبان is a coordinated noun phrase ('one heart and one tongue') meaning 'united/unanimous'; verb precedes the complement, a common option in poetic Persian.
segments.s745-g178: مگویید is also a negative imperative (do not say). کز (that) introduces the subordinate content clause. The subject of the subordinate clause is omitted (understood 'he'); شد is past tense 'became' and بدگمان is a predicate adjective 'suspicious'—together meaning 'became suspicious (of)'.
segments.s745-g179: وگر is a conditional conjunction ('if' / 'but if'). شد functions here as the past/become verb marking the hypothetical ('were/became'). همه is the universal 'all', and زیر یک چادر + -یم (چادریم) forms a prepositional phrase meaning 'under one tent' with an implied copula for 'we' (poetic compactness).
segments.s745-g180: به مردی is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in the manner/quality of a man' (i.e. 'as men' or 'with manliness'). همه is 'all'; یاد هم ('remembrance of one another') combined with دیگریم (a 1st‑person plural copular/nominal form) yields a poetic sense 'we are reminders of one another'—یاد functions here as a noun with reciprocal sense added by هم and an implied 'we are' in دیگریم.
segments.s745-g181: همان چون is a fixed poetic conjunction meaning 'as soon as' / 'when'. شنیدند is past tense, 3rd-person plural 'they heard'. آواز اوی is 'his voice' (آواز + اوی, the archaic/poetic third‑person pronoun).
segments.s745-g182: بدانست is a past tense verb 'knew it' / 'perceived it' (3rd‑person sing. used impersonally here). هر مهتری ('every/chief elder') follows in a fronted/marked position for emphasis—the noun phrase is the logical subject, and راز اوی ('his secret') is the object/thing perceived; اوی again is the archaic possessive/pronominal form 'his'.
segments.s745-g183: مهان (guests) is the subject. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'at once' / 'entirely'. از جای is a prepositional phrase 'from their place/seats' and برخاستند is the past 3rd‑person plural 'they rose'—standard S + adv + PP + verb ordering in poetry.
segments.s745-g184: بران is an adverb 'thereupon' / 'upon that'. هم is 'also/too'. نشان پاسخ is a compound noun phrase 'a sign/token of answer' (نشان 'sign', پاسخ 'answer/response'). آراستند is a past 3rd‑person plural verb meaning 'they prepared/arranged' (here: 'set forth' or 'presented').
segments.s745-g49: شهنشاه is the regal title 'the shah' (an older compound form). بنشست is a past tense verb 'sat down' (third person singular, classical/poetic past form). با مهتران is a prepositional phrase 'with the elders/chiefs' following the verb; note مهتران is a plural noun (poetic register) meaning leaders/elders.
segments.s745-g50: هر آنکس که بودند is a relative clause: هر آنکس 'all those / each one who' + که (relative 'who/that') + بودند (past plural 'they were'). ز ایران سران is a poetic order meaning 'the leaders of Iran' where ز ('from/of') links ایران to سران ('leaders'). Word order is slightly inverted for poetry but reads 'all those who were the leaders of Iran.'
segments.s745-g51: ز here is the poetic ablative 'from' (indicating source). اندیشه پاک‌دل is a noun phrase 'the thought/ mind of the pure‑hearted (one)' with پاک‌دل as a compound adjective 'pure‑hearted.' رابشست is the past verb 'washed' with the object marker را attached to the verb (classical/poetic orthography: را+بشست → رابشست), meaning 'washed/removed (it).'
segments.s745-g52: فراوان modifies the following phrase as 'many/abundant.' زهر گونه‌ای is 'poison of a kind' → 'various kinds of poison' (poetic grouping). چاره جست is a past simple verbal phrase 'sought a remedy' (جست here = 'sought' rather than literal 'leaped'). The subject is the same implied agent as the previous clause.
segments.s745-g53: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' اندیشه روشن آمد is 'a clear/bright thought came' (آمد is past 'came'). فراز here is an adverbial/postposed particle 'up/on high' or 'to the fore' — 'a clear thought rose up/occurred.' The phrase order (verb at end with postposed فراز) reflects classical/poetic syntax.
segments.s745-g54: یکی 'someone/a certain one' is the subject. نامه بنوشت is 'wrote a letter' — بنوشت is a poetic past form 'wrote' (here the verb takes the object نامه). نزد گراز is the prepositional phrase 'to/into the presence of the boar' (نزد = 'in the presence of / before').
segments.s745-g55: که introduces a content clause 'that.' از تو پسندیدم is 'I liked/approved (it) of you' — از تو indicates 'on your part' or 'from you' and پسندیدم is first‑person past 'I approved/liked.' این کارکرد is the direct object 'this deed/action' (این = 'this', کارکرد = 'deed/performance').
segments.s745-g56: ستودم is 'I praised' (first person past). تو را has the object pronoun 'you' plus the object marker را (here explicitly attached as a separate word), so 'I praised you.' نزد مردان مرد is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the men of men' — an emphatic idiom meaning 'before the bravest of men' (مردان = men/warriors, مرد = hero/valiant man used appositively).
segments.s745-g153: سپه (subject) precedes the past-tense verb رفت ('went/marched'). تا here marks the limit/destination (‘as far as/to’) and is followed by a toponymial phrase خره اردشیر, where خره functions as the place-name element modifying the proper name اردشیر (’Ardashir’). The clause is a simple past motion clause: 'the army went to Ardashir’s Khareh.'
segments.s745-g154: هر آنکس که introduces a universal relative clause ('every person who/that'). بودند is the past plural copula/verb agreeing with the implied plural sense ('they were'); برنا و پیر are coordinate predicate adjectives ('young and old') describing those people. The construction means 'everyone, whether young or old.'
segments.s745-g155: کشیدند is a past plural verb ('they drew/moved'); لشکر is the object/affected noun ('the army'). بران is a locative/adverbial pointing to that place ('to/onto that'), and رودبار is the place ('river-valley'). Together this yields 'they moved the army to that river-valley.'
segments.s745-g156: بدان here is 'to know/find out' (ب + دان) introducing purpose with تا ('so that/in order that') followed by an indirect question چه فرمان دهد شهریار — 'what the king will/order.' دهد is a present/subjunctive-like form used to report the king's ordering (future sense). The clause means 'in order to learn what the king would command.'
segments.s745-g157: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. آگاه شد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning 'became aware' (آگاه adjective + شد 'became') in past tense; خسرو is the subject. از کارشان is a prepositional phrase 'about/of their affair/deed' where شان is the 3rd-person plural suffix 'their.'
segments.s745-g158: نبود is the past negative copula 'was not' followed by the predicate آرزومند ('desirous/eager') and the object/phrase دیدارشان ('their meeting' = 'to see them'). Word order places the negative copula before the predicate, giving 'he was not eager for their meeting' (= 'he did not wish to see them').
segments.s745-g159: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces the subordinate/purpose clause. زاد فرخ here functions as a personal name (Zād‑Farrokh) split into two words in the text, and برفت is a past motion verb 'he went/shall go' (here subordinate clause after an order: 'that X go'). The clause thus means 'he ordered that Zād-Farrokh go.'
segments.s745-g160: به نزدیک is a locative phrase 'to/near the vicinity of'; آن لشکر is 'that army.' شاه is a genitive modifier 'of the king' (the army belonging to the king), and تفت appears as a following name/word attached to شاه (likely a name or epithet). The whole phrase functions as the destination of the previous verb: 'to the vicinity of that army of the king (Taft).'
segments.s745-g209: به دشنام is a prepositional/manner phrase meaning “with/by curses” (literally ‘to/with insult’). لب را is the definite object (را marks لب as the thing affected). گشایید is an imperative (2nd‑person plural/formal) of گشودن “to open.” باز follows the verb as an adverb/adjective meaning “open/wide/again,” completing the command “open your lips (wide) to curse.”
segments.s745-g210: The correlative چه ... چه ... means “whether ... or ...” (introducing two alternatives). بر is the preposition governing the following pronouns; بر من = 'against me' and بر شاه = 'against the king'. گردن‌فراز is a post‑nominal epithet modifying شاه, meaning “proud/lofty‑necked.”
segments.s745-g211: هر آنکس که introduces a general relative clause “whoever / anyone who.” بشنید is a past tense verb “heard.” ز + و (here written together as زو) is the preposition ز “from” plus the clitic pronoun و (= him), so زو = “from him.” این سخن = “this speech/these words,” the object of بشنید.
segments.s745-g212: بدانست is the main past verb “he/they knew” or “realized.” کان here introduces the content clause (classical/poetic usage) connecting to the following clause; its exact archaic sense is not one of the listed options. تخت نوشد کهن is the clause that follows: تخت “throne” is the subject, نوشد (poetic/past form) carries the meaning “was enjoyed / tasted” (i.e. ‘enjoyed’), and کهن “ancient/long ago” qualifies the idea — together giving “that the throne had long been enjoyed.”
segments.s745-g213: همه یکسر is a compound adverbial meaning “all at once / entirely.” از جای means “from their places” (from + place). برخاستند is the past plural verb “they rose / stood up,” so the clause says they all rose from their places at once.
segments.s745-g214: به دشنام here gives the manner “with curses / for the purpose of insulting.” لبها is the plural definite object “the lips.” بیاراستند is a past plural verb (bi‑آراستن) meaning “they prepared/arranged/adorned,” so together “they prepared their lips for cursing.”
segments.s745-g215: بشد is a narrative/formal particle meaning “it happened / then” (here functioning to introduce the action). زاد فرخ is a personal name (Zād‑Farrokh); in context it functions as the subject who acts. به خسرو = “to Khosrow” (prepositional phrase marking the addressee). بگفت is the past reported‑speech verb “he said.”
segments.s745-g216: که introduces the content of what was said: لشکر همه = “the whole army.” یار گشتند is a predicate in which گشتند (past plural of گشتن) means “became” and یار is predicate noun “all became allies/companions.” و جفت adds a coordinated predicate — جفت (= “paired / in pairs”) — so the clause reports that the army has become united as allies and paired off.
segments.s745-g185: This is an inverted, compressed clause meaning “a fortunate child was born to the king.” بر شاه marks the beneficiary/recipient (to the king); شد زاد is a periphrastic phrase ‘a birth happened/was born’ (شد as a verbal auxiliary), with فرخ as a post‑nominal adjective ‘fortunate.’ چو + گرد begins a temporal clause: چو = “when” and گرد (here an intransitive sense) = “went about/circulated,” linking to the next clause.
segments.s745-g186: سخنهای ایشان = “their words/speeches” (possessive noun phrase) and همه = “all.” یاد کرد is a light verbal construction where یاد (a noun ‘remembrance’) combines with کرد (the verbal auxiliary ‘did’) to yield the verb ‘remembered.’ Word order places the object/phrase before the verbal complex.
segments.s745-g187: بدو گفت = “he said to him” (بدو is the indirect‑object adverb ‘to him/unto him’ before the verb گفت). رو is an imperative/jussive ‘go’ (an archaic, short imperative), followed by پیش ایشان ‘in their presence/before them.’ بگوی here is a jussive/imperative form ‘tell’ or ‘say’ that introduces the content clause begun by که.
segments.s745-g188: که introduces the indirect question/command clause. اندر شما = ‘among you’ or ‘within you’ (setting the locus), کیست = interrogative ‘who is’ (کی + copula ست), and آزار جوی is a compound: آزار ‘harm/harassment’ + جوی ‘-seeker’ (an agentive ‘seeker’), i.e. ‘harasser’ or ‘one who seeks to do harm.’ The clause therefore asks who among you is the harasser.
segments.s745-g189: که introduces an exclamatory/conditional clause (here a kind of oath or curse). بفریفتش is a third‑person form with the object suffix –ش ‘him’ meaning ‘(that) he deceived/beguiled him.’ قیصر is the emperor. شوم is a jussive/subjunctive first‑person form ‘may I become’ (a formulaic curse), and بخت is the noun ‘fortune/fate’ governed by that wish — literally ‘may my fortune become ill.’
segments.s745-g190: A short, enumerative adjunct introduced by به and repeated for emphasis: به گنج و سلیح و به تاج و به تخت = ‘with respect to treasure and weapon(s), and with respect to crown and throne.’ These are the spheres in which the speaker wishes ill fortune; the repeated به is a poetic device listing items.
segments.s745-g191: که again introduces a report/explanation. نزدیک ما = ‘near us’ or ‘in our presence’ (a prepositional/locative phrase), او = ‘he,’ گنهکار شد = ‘became guilty/sinful’ (شد = became). The clause states that, in their presence or near them, he was declared guilty or proved a sinner.
segments.s745-g192: هم = ‘also.’ از + (tangible items) + بیزار شد = ‘became averse/loath to’ — here از تاج و اورنگ ‘from crown and worldly glory’ is the object of being averse. بیزار شد is a compound verbal phrase ‘became loath/turned away from.’ Word order places the objects before the verbal predicate, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s745-g193: فرستید is a past-tense verb 'sent' (classical narrative past; subject is implicit). یک سر here is idiomatic (one-go / at once) used adverbially, not a literal 'head'. بدین is the prepositional phrase 'to this' (ب- + this), and بارگاه is the goal 'court/palace'. The whole segment means 'sent at once to this court.'
segments.s745-g194: کسی را که begins a relative clause: 'someone whom/that…' را is the definite-object/relativizing marker attached to کسی; بودست is an archaic/past-form 'was'; زین is the fused preposition 'ز این' (here meaning 'of/from this') which is not one of the provided senses; سرگناه is a predicate meaning 'guilty (of the sin)'. Grammatically this clause modifies 'کسی' -> 'someone who was guilty of this.'
segments.s745-g195: بشد functions as a narrative 'it happened/then he rose' (a classical past/auxiliary sense). زاد فرخ is a compound personal name (Zad‑Farrokh); here the two nouns form the name/subject. بگفت is the past 'he said' and این سخن is the direct object 'these words/this speech.' The word order places the narrative verb before the name for poetic effect.
segments.s745-g196: رخ لشکر نو is a genitive-like sequence 'the face/appearance of the new army' (رخ = appearance/face; noun–noun apposition). ز (poetic for زِ/از) here means 'from' or 'because of', so 'ز غم' = 'from grief'. شد کهن is 'became old/aged'; the verb follows the cause phrase, producing 'their appearance became aged because of grief.'
segments.s745-g197: نیارست here expresses inability or that something did not happen (an archaic/poetic negative/resultive idea) — 'it did not come about that…'. لب را گشود is 'opened his lips' (لب = lip; را marks the object). ایچ کس is an emphatic negative 'no one' (ایچ = nothing/none; کس = person), so the whole segment means 'no one opened their lips.'
segments.s745-g198: پر از is the adjectival phrase 'full of' (پر = full), so پر از درد و خامش = 'full of pain and silence.' بماندند is past plural 'they remained' (stative past). و بس is a closing formula 'and that was enough/and that was all.' Together: 'they remained full of pain and silence, and that was all.'
segments.s745-g199: سبک زاد فرخ is an epithet + name: سبک‌زاد 'light‑born/quickly born' modifying the name زاد فرخ (Zad‑Farrokh). زبان برگشاد is a compact clause 'opened his tongue' (زبان = tongue/speech; برگشاد = opened, past participle used verbally). The word order places the epithet before the name in typical poetic fashion.
segments.s745-g200: همی‌کرد is the imperfective/iterative past marker 'was doing / began to' used here to introduce reported speech. گفتار ناخوب is the object 'speech/words that are bad' (harsh words). یاد here is a noun 'remembrance/mention' used idiomatically with گفتار to mean 'utter/bring to mind' (i.e., 'began to utter/recall harsh words').
segments.s745-g217: مرا بیم جانست is a nominal clause meaning 'there is fear to/for me' → 'I fear for my life.' Here م + را together function as the object/dative 'مرا' ('to me'), followed by the noun بیم 'fear' and the copular/adjectival phrase جانست ('is life' used idiomatically: 'a danger to life' / 'fear for life'). اگر نیز شاه begins the conditional clause 'if also the king...': اگر = 'if', نیز = 'also/yet' attached to شاه 'king'. Word order is typical classical Persian: experiencer ('مرا') + noun ('بیم') + copula ('جانست'), then the conditional marker and subject of the subordinate clause.
segments.s745-g218: فرستد is the verb of the conditional clause 'if ... sends' (third person singular; here conditional/subjunctive sense: 'if he sends'). The following prepositional phrase به پیغام denotes purpose or means ('with a message' / 'on a message'), and نزد سپاه means 'to the presence of the army' / 'to the army.' The direct object (a messenger) is omitted; Persian often leaves the agent/recipient implicit.
segments.s745-g219: بدانست is a past-tense verb 'he knew' placed before its subject (verb–subject order is common in classical Persian). خسرو is the subject 'Khosrow.' که introduces the content clause 'that ...' and آن کژگوی 'that liar' is the subject of the subordinate clause.
segments.s745-g220: همی is a classical adverb here giving a sense of continuous or emphatic action ('would surely/continually'); the clause then lists objects 'آب و خون' ('water and blood') followed by the locative phrase 'اندر آرد به جوی' where آرد (brings/pours) is the verb at the end. اندر = 'into/within', and به جوی = 'into the stream.' The omitted subject is the 'کژگوی' from the previous segment.
segments.s745-g221: ز بیم برادرش = 'from/because of fear of his brother' (ز = poetic 'from/because of'); ش is the possessive suffix on برادر 'his brother.' چیزی نگفت is a negative past clause: چیزِی 'anything/something' + نگفت 'did not say' (ن- negative prefix + گفت 'said'), so 'he said nothing.' Word order places the reason before the verb.
segments.s745-g222: همی‌داشت is an imperfect/progressive past auxiliary phrase 'he kept / was keeping.' آن راستی 'that truth' is the object, and در نهفت is a locative/explanatory phrase 'in concealment / hidden.' Literally 'he was keeping that truth hidden.' The compound shows auxiliary (داشت) + participial/adjectival complement (نهفت) in classical usage.
segments.s745-g223: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that.' پیچیده is a past participle/verb meaning 'turned/twisted/withdrew' in this context (classical poetic usage can be figurative), modified by بد 'badly/angrily' and followed by the subject رستم 'Rostam.' از شهریار indicates movement away 'from the king.' Word order places the verb before its modifiers and subject, a common classical pattern.
segments.s745-g224: بجایی خود = 'to his own place' or 'to his proper place' (بـ + جایی: to/place; خود emphasizes 'his own'). The conjunction و 'and' links the following noun phrase تیغ‌زن ده هزار = 'ten thousand sword‑wielders' (تیغ‌زن 'swordsman; fighter', with the numeral phrase 'ده هزار' 'ten thousand' following). The whole phrase describes Rostam withdrawing to his place accompanied by ten thousand swordsmen.
segments.s745-g57: ز کردارها is an ablative/prepositional phrase 'by/from the deeds' (ز = 'by/from'). برفزودی is a past tense (classical) verb meaning 'you/one increased' formed from بر + افزود (here used as a 2nd/3rd‑person past in poetic diction) and precedes its object (poetic word order). فریب is a noun 'deceit/deception' that functions as the object of برفزودی.
segments.s745-g58: This clause contains the verb آوردی 'you brought' (second‑person past/poetic) with its object سر قیصر 'Caesar's head' placed before the verb; اندر نشیب is an adverbial phrase 'into the descent/low place' (اندر = 'in/into'). The possessor relationship (head of Caesar) is expressed by the noun pair 'سر قیصر' with no separate genitive marker.
segments.s745-g59: چواین is the fused temporal conjunction 'چو + این' meaning 'when/when this'; نامه آرند is 'they bring the letter' (آرند = 'bring' in a classical form, here 3rd‑person plural), and نزدیک تو 'near you/to you' is the locative/adverbial complement placed after the verb.
segments.s745-g60: پراندیشه کن is an imperative/hortative phrase: پراندیشه 'full of thought/considerate' combined with the imperative کن 'do/Be —' → 'be thoughtful/consider carefully.' رای تاریک تو is a noun phrase 'your dark/secret counsel' (رای = 'counsel/plan', تاریک here = 'obscure/secret', and تو is the possessive pronoun placed after the noun as in classical Persian).
segments.s745-g61: همی‌باش is an imperative 'stay/remain' in archaic/poetic diction addressing the addressee. تا introduces a subordinate temporal clause 'until', here followed by من بجنبم 'I move/stir' — بجنبم is a present/subjunctive form 'I move' used for the forthcoming action. زجای is the prepositional phrase 'from (the) place' (ز + جای) whose possessor is postponed to the next segment.
segments.s745-g62: تو is the (implied) addressee; با لشکر خویش is a prepositional phrase 'with your own army' (با = 'with', لشکر = 'army', خوی + ش = خویش 'own/one's own' formed by the stem خوی + clitic ش). بگذار is an imperative 'put/place' (here idiomatically 'set/advance') and پای 'foot' is its object — 'set foot' = 'advance/move forward.'
segments.s745-g63: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when.' زین روی و زان روی is a classical locative formula meaning 'on this side and on that side' (روی = 'side/surface'; زین/زان are archaic deictics 'this/that' attached to روی). باشد is a classical present/subjunctive copula 'is/will be' and سپاه 'army' is the subject; the clause thus means 'when an army is on this side and that.'
segments.s745-g64: شود is a verbal auxiliary 'becomes/will be' (here introducing the result), در سخن is an adverbial 'in speech/in talk,' رای قیصر is a possessed noun phrase 'Caesar's counsel' (رای = 'counsel/plan' followed by the possessor قیصر), and تباه is the predicate adjective 'ruined/undone.' The word order places the verb before the adverbial and predicate, typical of the poem's style.
segments.s745-g65: This clause has a light prepositional phrase 'به ایران' ('to/into Iran') followed by the verb phrase 'دستگیر آوریم'. The object marker 'را' (placed after 'و' here in the text layout) marks the (previously mentioned) direct object; 'دستگیر' is an attributive/participial adjective 'seized' used with the verb آوریم ('we will bring'), where آوریم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive/optative form used here to express a future/intent ('we will/let us bring').
segments.s745-g66: This coordinate clause repeats the verb آوریم with its object explicitly: 'همه رومیان را اسیر آوریم' = 'we will bring all the Romans captive.' 'همه رومیان' is the definite plural object, marked by 'را', and 'اسیر' is a predicative/attributive noun/adjective ('captive'); آوریم again expresses the group's intended action.
segments.s745-g67: The preposition 'ز' (poetic for 'از') means 'from' and governs 'درگه' ('the court'). 'یکی' here is the indefinite 'a certain' (not the numeral), modifying 'چاره‌گر' ('one who finds solutions'); 'برگزید' is simple past 'he chose/selected.' Word order is preposition + place + indefinite + noun + verb (typical in classical Persian).
segments.s745-g68: This is a descriptive apposition: two coordinating epithets describing the chosen man. 'سخن‌دان' = 'one knowledgeable in speech' (an eloquent speaker), 'و' links to 'گویا' where 'گویا' here is an adjectival sense 'eloquent' rather than its epistemic sense. 'چناچون سزید' is a compact clause: 'چناچون' = 'such/as such' and 'سزید' is the past of 'سزیدن' used impersonally to mean 'it was fitting' or 'befitted him.' The whole phrase means 'an eloquent, persuasive man, as befit(ted).'
segments.s745-g69: 'بدو گفت' is the common classical sequence 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'). 'کاین نامه اندر نهان' is an object clause introduced by 'ک' (= 'that/that this'): 'that this letter (کاین نامه) in secret/hidden (اندر نهان) ...' Note word order: indirect object 'بدو' comes before the verb گفت, and the subordinate clause follows.
segments.s745-g70: 'همی' here is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/just'); 'بر' functions as 'to' or 'upon' introducing the beneficiary/recipient; 'بکردار' is an adjectival phrase 'of good/virtuous conduct' used substantively to mean a worthy/able person; 'کارآگهان' (plural or collective-looking form) means 'people informed/aware' or 'a person skilled in affairs.' Together the phrase directs: 'give it to a discreet, capable doer.'
segments.s745-g71: 'چنان کن' is an imperative: 'do so / make it so.' The conjunction 'که' introduces the result/conditional clause ('so that' / 'if'), and 'رومیت بیند کسی' literally 'if any Roman sees (it)' — 'بیند' is the present/subjunctive used in classical Persian to express potential/conditional 'should see' or 'sees.'
segments.s745-g72: 'بره' here is metaphorical: 'a lamb' = 'an innocent/naïve person.' 'بر' with 'سخن' means 'about the matter/speech' (literally 'upon the speech'), and 'پرسد' is the present/subjunctive/optative 3rd-person form 'will ask' (classical usage for prospective action). 'از تو بسی' = 'much/from you' — i.e. 'will ask you many questions.' Word order places the questioner subject ('بره') before the verb.
segments.s745-g129: Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: the prepositional phrase 'به قیصر' ('to the Caesar') comes first, followed by the adverbial 'بسی' ('much/very'), the verb 'کرد' ('made/did') and the object 'پوزش' ('apology'); the subject 'گراز' ('the boar') appears at the end, typical of Persian SOV but here after the verb for emphasis. 'بسی کرد پوزش' = 'made many/ample apologies.'
segments.s745-g130: 'به کوشش' is an ablative/instrumental phrase meaning 'by/with effort'; 'نیامد' is the past negative of آمدن ('did not come') and here functions idiomatically as 'did not (cause to) come / did not fall (into)'; 'بدامش' = 'into his snare' (بدام + -ش = 'to/in his trap'); 'فراز' is a short adverbial/noun at the end meaning 'above/up/high' or a final intensifier — here it remains adverbial, so the full sense is 'by effort he did not come into his snare (up/on it)'.
segments.s745-g131: The verb 'گزین کرد' (chose) appears before the subject 'خسرو' by inversion for poetic rhythm; 'پس' functions as a temporal adverb 'then'; 'آزاده‌ای' is the object meaning 'a noble/free-hearted one' (the -ای ending marks an indefinite person).
segments.s745-g132: This is a nominal phrase describing the envoy: 'سخن گوی' = 'speaker' or 'eloquent (one who speaks)' (compound of 'سخن' + 'گوی'), 'و دانا' = 'and wise', and 'فرستاده‌ای' literally 'a sent one' (i.e. an envoy). The adjectives/compound precede the noun in a poetic listing.
segments.s745-g133: 'یکی' ('a certain' / 'someone') is the indefinite subject; 'نامه بنوشت' is an archaic/past verbal form meaning 'wrote a letter' (بنوشت = نوشت); 'سوی' is the directional preposition 'toward/to', and 'گراز' is the addressee. Word order here is Subject – Object – Verb (classical) with the direction phrase following.
segments.s745-g134: This clause is the opening of the quoted address. 'که‌ای' functions as the particle introducing direct speech/vocative (essentially 'O...'); 'بی‌بها' is an epithet literally 'without price' (here 'priceless' or possibly ironic); 'ریمن' is a proper name; 'دیو ساز' is a compound meaning 'maker/forger of demons' (دیو 'demon' + ساز 'maker/devise' used nominally). This is a vocative formula addressing the named person.
segments.s745-g135: 'تو را' places 'you' as the object (را is the definite/object marker); 'چند خوانم' is a rhetorical question 'how long / how many times shall I call/summon' with 'خوانم' (first-person present/subjunctive of خواندن used as 'call/summon'); 'برین بارگاه' = 'to that high/exalted court' (بر + این → برین as a fused demonstrative 'that above').
segments.s745-g136: 'همی' is a present/continuous marker meaning 'still' or 'will continue to'; 'دورمانی' is a noun/adjective meaning 'your remaining distant' or 'being kept away'; 'ز' = 'from'; 'فرمان' = 'command/sovereign authority'; 'و راه' = 'and the way/road' (i.e. the path or rightful route). Together: 'you will remain far from authority and the path.'
segments.s745-g137: کنون = 'now' (temporal adverb) sets the time. آن سپاهی is a nominal phrase 'that army/host'. که introduces a relative clause describing the host. نزد here means 'in the presence of / near (someone)'; تواند is a third-person singular present/potential 'is able' or 'can'. So the clause literally says 'that army which can be near (in the presence [of someone])'.
segments.s745-g138: بسال و به ماه is a temporal phrase 'for a year and for a month' (lit. 'in/for a year and in/for a month'). اورمزد (Ahura Mazda) is the subject of the following تواند, again 'can / is able' — here indicating what Auramazd can do regarding that host (classical text sometimes leaves the precise verb object implicit). Word order places the time phrase before the divine subject for emphasis.
segments.s745-g139: به رای و به دل is a pair of prepositional phrases 'in counsel and in heart' (lit. 'to/for opinion and to/for heart'), functioning adverbially. ویژه is an adjective 'special/dedicated' modifying the implied people; با + قیصرند = 'they are with Caesar' (با = 'with', قیصرند is the fused form with the third-person plural copula ending, meaning 'they are (with) Caesar'). The overall sense: in outward counsel and feeling they are devoted to Caesar.
segments.s745-g140: نهانی is an adverb 'secretly/ covertly' modifying the clause. به اندیشه = 'in thought' or 'toward thought' (here 'in their thought' = 'in intention'). دیگرند is a predicate 'they are other/different' meaning 'they are of other mind' — so the clause says 'secretly they are of other thought.' The negative/contrast between the two segments is a common rhetorical structure.
segments.s745-g141: برما فرست is an imperative construction: برما = 'to us' (prepositional phrase), فرست = 'send' (imperative or hortative). آنک is a relative pronoun 'those who/that which', and پیچیده‌اند is perfect tense 'they have conspired / they have woven (a plot)' — here 'پیچیدن' is used idiomatically for plotting or gathering together.
segments.s745-g142: همه = 'all/everyone' serving as subject/quantifier. سرکشی را بسی چیده‌اند is here written together as رابسیچیده‌اند; grammatically it is را + بسی + چیده‌اند = 'have very much arranged/collected/put in order' of 'سرکشی' (rebellion). In other words 'they have together mustered/organized rebellion.' Note old poetic concatenation (را + بسی + چیده‌اند) is written as one word here.
segments.s745-g143: چواین is the fused form of چو + این 'when this' (temporal conjunction). نامه آمد is simple past 'the letter came'. بنزد = 'to the presence of / before' (older prepositional use). گراز is a noun 'graz/boar' here serving as the person/figure to whose presence the letter came (proper name or epithet).
segments.s745-g144: پر اندیشه شد is the common construction 'پر + noun + شدن' = 'to become full of [noun]' so پر اندیشه شد = 'became full of thought / grew thoughtful.' کهتر دیوساز is a noun phrase 'کهتر (the lesser/meaner) دیوساز (demon‑maker/sorcerer)' — an epithet or reference that he thought about. Word order places the predicate 'شد' after the adjective phrase, typical of Classical Persian.
segments.s745-g105: و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” زان (from that) + جایگه (place) is a prepositional phrase “from that place.” لشکر is the subject “the army,” اندر is an older/prepositional adverb meaning “into/within,” and کشید is the past of کشیدن “to draw/move” used here intransitively to mean the army moved/drew in. Word order is verb-final for the verbal element (لشکر اندر کشید).
segments.s745-g106: شد is the past/copulative ‘became/was’ marking the change of state. آن آرزو ‘that desire/longing’ is the subject; بر دلش is a locative phrase ‘on/in his heart’ (بر used in classical Persian for the mental locus). ناپدید is the predicate adjective ‘vanished/unseen.’ The construction literally: ‘that desire became vanished on his heart.’
segments.s745-g107: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” آگاهی is the noun “news/information” and آمد is the past “came/arrived”; به سوی is the prepositional phrase “to/toward,” here marking the recipient of the news. In classical style the recipient (گراز) can be a proper name; the clause reads ‘when news came to Goraz.’
segments.s745-g108: که introduces a subordinate (reported) clause ‘that …’. آن نامور is ‘that renowned/famed one’ (آن as determiner). شد is the past/copulative ‘became/was’ and is followed by a directional phrase سوی روم باز meaning ‘returned toward Rome’ (سوی + روم) with باز as the adverb ‘again.’ Overall: ‘that the famed one was again toward Rome/returned to Rome.’
segments.s745-g109: دلش ‘his heart’ (possessive -ش) is the topic. گشت is the past of گشتن used as ‘became/turned.’ پر + درد is an adjectival phrase ‘full of pain’ (پر governs a following noun: ‘full (of) pain’). The conjunction و links the second predicative phrase رخساره زرد ‘his visage/complexion pale,’ where the adjective follows the noun in typical Persian order.
segments.s745-g110: سواری is the direct object ‘a rider/horseman.’ گزید is the past of گزیدن ‘chose/selected’ (transitive); the subject is understood from the previous clause (he/the king). ازدلیران is a genitive phrase ‘from the brave (ones)’ (از + دلیران), and مرد here apposes or reinforces the human/valiant sense ‘man/hero.’
segments.s745-g111: یکی is the indefinite ‘a certain/one.’ نامه بنوشت is the past construction ‘wrote a letter’; بنوشت is an archaic/poetic past form of نوشتن (here treated as ‘wrote’). با باد و دم is an instrumental phrase ‘by/with wind and breath’ (idiomatic for sending quickly or by the air). Word order places the manner/instrument after the verb phrase.
segments.s745-g112: که introduces the content of the letter (an indirect question/report). بر من is ‘toward me / against me’ (classical use of بر for direction/affecting someone). چرا precedes the verb as the interrogative ‘why,’ گشت is ‘became/turned,’ and قیصر دژم ‘the Caesar [became] sullen/hostile’ with دژم as a postpositive adjective describing the emperor’s mood.
segments.s745-g73: بگیرد is a third-person potential/subjunctive form of گرفتن (literary/classical b‑prefix) meaning “may seize / might take.” The object تو را (تو + accusative marker را) follows the verb stem and marks “you” as the direct object. نزد قیصر is a prepositional phrase “in the presence of / before Caesar,” and the clause ends with برد, here the main verb meaning “carry / take” (an archaic/past form placed at the end as usual in Persian); the subject is implicit (he/they).
segments.s745-g74: گرت is an archaic conditional particle (contraction related to اگر) meaning “if.” The phrase نزد سالار لشکر “before the commander of the army” is the locative/purposive complement; برد is the clause-final verb “takes/carries” with the same implicit subject as the previous clause. Note that تو را (your object) is omitted here but understood from the previous segment (ellipsis common in Persian).
segments.s745-g75: بپرسد is a third-person narrative/potential form of پرسیدن (‘he may ask’ / ‘he asks’). The object marker تو را marks “you” as the one being asked. کز کجایی is an embedded interrogative “from where” (کز = “from which / from”), and the negative imperative مگوی (“do not say / do not tell”) addresses the addressee — verb at the end, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s745-g76: بگویش is an imperative/optative form meaning “tell him” (بگو + ـش = to him). که introduces reported speech (“that…”). The copula is omitted, so من کهتری چاره‌جوی literally lists subject + adjective + noun: “I [am] a lesser/humble (کهتری) seeker of remedy/help (چاره‌جوی).” The phrase functions as the content to be told to the asker.
segments.s745-g77: The phrase begins with به which here functions as the old/preverbal particle (written out in poetry) attached to the verb; پیمودم is a past simple 1st‑person singular ‘I traversed/underwent’ (پیمودن). The following noun group این رنج راه دراز is a clustered object: این = 'this', رنج = 'hardship/suffering' and راه دراز = 'the long road' (راه + post‑positive adjective دراز); Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe in older/poetic usage, so read it as “this hardship of the long road.”
segments.s745-g78: یکی is numeral/indefinite 'one/a single' modifying نامه; نامه دارم is present‑tense literal 'I have a letter' (دارم = I have). بسوی is a preposition 'toward' that takes the following noun phrase as its goal; گراز is the intended recipient/target. Word order is subjectless for the possession clause (literal: 'one letter I have toward Gāraz').
segments.s745-g79: تواین is the fused demonstrative phrase تو این (= 'in/this very'), here introducing the direct object 'this letter' (تواین نامه = 'this very letter'). بربند is an imperative or hortative form 'bind/fasten/wrap' addressed implicitly to 'you' (the imperative verb often appears without an overt pronoun). بردست is a locative phrase 'into/into the hand' (بردست = 'to the hand' or 'in the hand'), and راست qualifies it as the right hand ('راست' = 'right'). The verb governing these commands is understood as the imperative in بربند.
segments.s745-g80: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدون is an older/poetic present/imperfect form meaning 'he/one comes' (a third‑person verb form). که here introduces the subordinate clause 'that he take (it)'; بستاند would be a subjunctive/optative‑style form 'he should take / may take' (the lexical senses listed in the input don't match this usage, so the exact lemma sense is irregular in the provided list). از تو = 'from you' (i.e. 'from your hand'). رواست is composed of روا 'permissible/fitting' plus the copula ست (poetic contraction of است), so 'it is permissible.' The whole conditional reads: 'If he comes, that he take it from you is permissible.'
segments.s745-g113: از ایران چرا بازگشتی بگوی is an imperative request: بگوی (imperative 'tell' / 'say') governs the preceding content clause. از ایران = 'from Iran' (prepositional phrase). چرا is the interrogative 'why' modifying بازگشتی. بازگشتی is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person past form ('you returned') placed before the speech-verb; the whole string means 'Tell (me) why you returned from Iran.'
segments.s745-g114: مرا کردی اندر جهان چاره‌جوی contains the result clause. م + را together form the object 'mara' = 'me' (here split in the manuscript). کردی is simple past 2nd person ('you made' / 'you caused'); اندر جهان is the locative 'in the world' (اندر is the older poetic preposition); چاره‌جوی is a predicative compound 'seeker of remedy' (چاره + جوی). Word order (object + verb + locative + predicate) is typical of Ferdowsi's poetic phrasing: 'you made me, in the world, a seeker of remedy.'
segments.s745-g115: شهنشاه داند که من کردم این is a main clause + subordinate clause. شهنشاه is the subject 'the king/emperor'. داند is the archaic/polite present 3rd-person of 'know' (in prose modern would be می‌داند); که introduces the subordinate statement. من کردم این is verb-final poetic word order: کردم (past 1st person 'I did/made') with من subject and این ('this') as the object/emphatic demonstrative after the verb.
segments.s745-g116: دلش گردد از من پر از درد وکین describes the consequence. دل‌ش (دل + enclitic ش) = 'his heart'. گردد is the present/subjunctive/poetic 3sg of 'become' (گشت/گردد) meaning 'will become' or 'may become'. از من here gives cause/agency ('because of me' / 'on my account'). پر از ... is the idiom 'full of ...' where پر is 'full' and از introduces the content (درد و کین = 'pain and hatred').
segments.s745-g117: چو قیصر نگه کرد و آن نامه دید begins with the temporal conjunction چو = 'when'. قیصر is the subject. نگه کرد is a verbal compound meaning 'he looked' (نگه + کرد, past 3sg). و links the action to a second verb: آن نامه دید = 'he saw that letter' (دید past 3sg). The sequence gives two successive past actions.
segments.s745-g118: ز لشکر گرانمایه‌ای برگزید: ز (poetic variant of از) introduces the source 'from'. لشکر گرانمایه‌ای = 'an esteemed / noble army' (گرانمایه 'high-value, esteemed', -ای برای نامتعین/حداقلی). برگزید is past 3sg 'he chose/selected' — the object (troops/men) is implied; literal: 'he selected from the noble army.'
segments.s745-g119: فرستاد تازان به نزد گراز: فرستاد is past 3sg 'he sent/dispatch(ed)'. تازان (plural) are 'raiders/cavalrymen' (an ethnic/military group or fierce horsemen). به نزد is a poetic locative construction meaning 'to the presence of / to the place of', followed by گراز which may be a proper name (Gôraz) or the noun 'boar' — here it functions as the recipient/location. The direct object (تازان) precedes the prepositional phrase.
segments.s745-g120: کزان ایزدت کرده‌بد بی‌نیاز is a relative/result clause modifying the previous noun: کزان = 'from whom / from which'. ایزدت = ایزد + -ت 'your God' or 'God for you' (ایزد = deity/Providence, -ت 2nd-person clitic). کرده‌بد is a poetic/irregular perfective construction (here to be read as 'has caused/made' in context), and بی‌نیاز is the resultant adjective 'free of need, self-sufficient'. Together: 'from whom God has made you free (of want).' Note: some forms are archaic/poetic and the manuscript division causes unusual morphology.
segments.s745-g89: ازو 'from/by him' is a prepositional pronoun (here marking the cause or object of astonishment). خیره شد is a compound past: خیره (astonished/staring) + شد (become) = 'was astonished/was struck dumb'; کهتر is a noun 'the inferior/one of lower rank' and چاره جوی is a compound noun (چاره = remedy + جوی = seeker) 'one seeking a remedy'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: the cause (ازو) + state (خیره شد) comes before introducing the person (کهتر) who is the seeker.
segments.s745-g90: ز بیمش is a causal phrase 'because of his fear' (ز = because of; بیمش = his fear). بپاسخ here functions as a response/answer notion ('in answer' / 'to answer'), followed by دژم کرد روی literally 'made a sullen face' — کرد is a light verb 'make/do' taking روی 'face' as its object and دژم (sullen) as the property applied; the sequence means 'he answered and made his face sullen' (i.e. answered sullenly).
segments.s745-g91: بجویید is a jussive/imperative-form verb 'seek!' (plural/polite), here functioning as quoted or reported speech; گفت is the speech-verb 'said' used immediately after the quoted imperative (a frequent classical order). این بلاجوی را is the direct object with را marking/defining 'this seeker of calamity' (بلاجوی = 'one who seeks/causes calamity').
segments.s745-g92: A simple appositive list of epithets linked by و (and): بداندیش 'ill‑minded/malicious', بدکام 'ill‑fated/unsatisfied', بدگوی 'ill‑speaking/slanderer'. The final را repeats the object-marker to topicalize/emphasize the whole coordinated phrase — common in older Persian poetics.
segments.s745-g93: بجستند is past tense 'they sought' (3rd pl). و is the simple conjunction 'and'. آن نامه از دست اوی literally 'that letter from his hand' uses از to mark source ('from') and اوی as a possessive pronoun 'his' (دست اوی = 'his hand'). The phrase describes finding/taking the letter from his hand.
segments.s745-g94: گشاد here is the past action 'opened' (or literally 'made open') taking as its complement the clause introduced by آنک. آنک is a relative/complementizer 'that/which' introducing the content of the letter. دانا بد و راه جوی are predicative adjectives/epithets applied inside that content: 'the wise (دانا) [was] bad (بد) and a seeker of ways (راه جوی)'. In classical Persian the copula is often omitted, so adjectives follow directly to predicate the subject introduced by آنک.
segments.s745-g95: ازان is a compound preposition 'from that/thereupon' marking origin. مرز gives the locality 'frontier/boundary' and دانا modifies the agent as 'the wise (one)'. سری را is the definite/emphatic object (سری = 'a secret' or 'hidden thing' + را as object marker). بجست is the past tense 'he sought' (3rd s.).
segments.s745-g96: که here introduces purpose or result ('so that' / 'in order that' or can be read as a relative 'who/that'); آن پهلوانی is 'that champion/hero' (آن = that). بخواند is a 3rd‑person form with the prefix ب‑ used for subjunctive/optative sense in classical Persian — 'may call/summon' or 'would call' — so the clause reads as his purpose/result. درست is an adverb 'correctly/properly' modifying بخواند.
segments.s745-g81: برون آمد is a past-tense verbal phrase meaning 'came out / emerged' (verb after the adverb). از پیش + [proper name] means 'from the presence of / out from before' someone; the proper name نوند follows خسرو and identifies who came out. Word order places the directional phrase before the subject/name.
segments.s745-g82: به بازو مر is an older possessive/dative construction: بازو 'arm' followed by the enclitic pronoun مر functioning as 'my'/'to me' (archaic). آن نامه را is the definite object (را marks the object), and کرد بند is a light verb construction where کرد (past 'did/made') combines with the noun بند ('a binding, bond/tie') to mean 'bound/tied.'
segments.s745-g83: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came.' چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when.' نزدیک قیصر رسید is a following clause with نزدیک 'near' and رسید ('arrived/reached') — together 'when he drew near and reached the Caesar.'
segments.s745-g84: یکی مرد is 'a certain man' (indefinite subject). بطریق here means 'on the road / in the roadway' or 'along the way.' او را بدید is the verb phrase 'saw him' with او را (him + object marker را) and بدید the past of 'see.'
segments.s745-g85: سوی + noun is a directional phrase 'toward.' قیصرش is the title with an enclitic pronoun (the suffix -ش), commonly used in classical Persian to identify the person referred to. برد is here a past verb 'bore/took/turned' (past of بردن) governing سر 'head' as its object; پر ز گرد is a follow-up adjectival phrase: پر 'full (of)' + ز (from/of) + گرد 'dust.' (Note: the provided sense-list for برد did not include the verb sense, so that entry is handled below.)
segments.s745-g86: دو رخ 'two cheeks' = 'both cheeks' followed by the adjective زرد 'pale/wan.' و links clauses. لبها شده لاژورد is a perfective/result phrase: شده (become) with the predicative noun لاژورد 'lapis-lazuli/azure' — 'his lips had become lapis-blue.'
segments.s745-g87: بدو is a prepositional/indirect-object form 'to him.' گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (the supplied senses did not list the speech-verb sense). قیصر is the agent/topic; که + clause introduces the indirect question/quoted content — خسرو کجاست = 'where is Khosrow' (کجاست is the enclitic copula ست attached to کجا 'where').
segments.s745-g88: ببایدت is an archaic impersonal + enclitic meaning 'it is necessary for you' (ببايد + ت). گفتن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to tell' (used as the complement of the necessity), بما 'to us' (dative), and راه راست is a noun + adjective pair meaning 'the right/correct way' (راه 'way' + راست 'right/correct'). The whole phrase literally: 'It is necessary for you to tell us the right way.'
segments.s745-g145: گزین کرد is a compound past: گزین (stem of گزیدن 'to choose') plus کرد 'did', yielding 'chose/selected.' زان is the archaic/prepositional form (به + آن) meaning 'from those' or 'from that,' and نامداران سوار is a noun phrase 'renowned horsemen/riders' (نامداران modifies سوار). Word order here is verb then its source phrase (he chose from those famed riders).
segments.s745-g146: از introduces the source 'from Iran and Niran' (ایران و نیران). The sequence ده و دو هزار is a traditional enumerative phrase giving the number (here to be read as twelve thousand). In this clause the prepositional phrase 'از ایران و نیران' specifies origin of the riders and the following numerals state how many.
segments.s745-g147: بدان is the older contracted form of به آن / به+آن meaning 'to those' or 'to that one' and functions as an indirect-object introducer ('to those elders'). گفت is the past 'he said' introducing direct speech. یک دل شوید is an exhortation: یک دل (one-hearted, of one mind) + شوید (subjunctive/imperative 2nd-person plural of شدن 'become'), so 'be of one heart' or 'be united.'
segments.s745-g148: سخن گفتن is the verbal-noun/infinitive phrase 'speaking' or 'words said.' هرکسی is an indefinite 'whomever/anyone.' مشنوید is a negative present/imperative (2nd-person plural) meaning 'do not listen' or 'do not heed.' The clause literally orders: 'Do not listen to the speech/words of anyone' — i.e. don't accept every person's plea or argument.
segments.s745-g149: بباشید is the imperative/plural form of بودن 'be/remain' ('stay, be you'). یک چند is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning 'for a little while' or 'somewhat.' زین is the noun 'saddle' and روی آب literally 'upon/at the water' (روی = upon/on, آب = water); together this phrase gives a locational/poetic image (stay a bit at/on the water or by your saddles) — the exact idiomatic reading is elliptical, but grammatically it places the time/setting for the order to remain.
segments.s745-g150: مگیرید here functions as a negative/hortative present (literally 'do not take' / 'do not employ/take up'), followed by یک سر ('even for an instant' / 'at all' — lit. 'one head' used adverbially) and the infinitival phrase به رفتن (towards going) plus شتاب (noun 'haste'). Together the clause means 'do not take even a moment to hasten in going' — i.e. 'do not rush off.'
segments.s745-g151: چو is a conditional/temporal conjunction 'when/if.' هم پشت باشید literally 'be (of) one back' (پشت = back/support; هم = together), an idiom meaning 'stand together/back-to-back' or 'support each other.' با همرهان 'with your companions' (با = with, همرهان = companions). The clause sets a conditional: 'If/when you stand united with your comrades...'
segments.s745-g152: یکی here functions adverbially 'as one' or 'together.' کوه کندن is the verbal phrase 'to pull up / take away a mountain' (کوه = mountain; کندن = to pull up/extract), and ز بن بر is a prepositional/locative sequence 'from the root/up from the foundation' (ز = from, بن = root/base, بر = up/upon — poetic word order). توان is the modal auxiliary 'can' (ability) placed at the clause end. Together: 'as one you can uproot a mountain from its base.'
segments.s745-g121: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that' (introducing what follows). 'ویران کنی' is a two-part verbal phrase: 'ویران' (root meaning 'to ruin') plus the present/subjunctive 2nd-person form 'کنی' (from کردن) = 'you destroy/lay waste'. 'تاج و گاه مرا' is the object: 'تاج' = 'crown' and 'گاه' = 'seat/place' combined, with the possessive suffix '-م' (here separated in the token list) = 'my', and 'را' the definite/object marker marking the whole phrase as the direct object of 'ویران کنی'.
segments.s745-g122: 'به آتش' marks the instrument/medium ('into/with fire'); 'بسوزی' is parallel to 'کنی' — the present/subjunctive (2nd person) of 'سوختن/سوزاندن', 'you burn' (optative/intentive sense here). 'سپاه مرا' = 'my army/host' with the possessive '-م' = 'my' and 'را' again the object marker; word order is instrumental phrase + verb + object (classical poetic order).
segments.s745-g123: 'کز آن نامه' = 'from that letter' (کز = 'from which/of which'). 'جز گنج دادن' = 'except the giving of treasure' (جز = 'except', گنج دادن = the act/infinitive 'to give treasure' treated nominally). 'بباد' is an archaic stative/passive-like verb 'be blown away' / 'be lost' describing the fate of that giving (elliptically: it was blown away/was lost).
segments.s745-g124: 'نیامد' is simple past negative 'did not come' (3rd person singular); 'مرا' (here split into tokens) functions as the dative/ethical object 'to me' ('it did not come to me'). 'از تو' = 'from you'. 'ای بد نژاد' is a vocative address 'O ill-born one' (تو + vocative 'ای', بد = 'bad/ill', نژاد = 'race/lineage').
segments.s745-g125: 'مرا خواستی' = 'you desired/wanted me' (مرا = 'me' as object; خواستی = 2nd person past 'you wanted'). 'تا' here means 'so that / in order that' and introduces a purpose/result clause. 'به خسرو دهی' = 'you would give (me) to Khosrow' where 'دهی' is present/subjunctive 2nd person 'you give' (complement of خواستی) and 'به خسرو' marks the recipient 'to Khosrow'.
segments.s745-g126: 'که' introduces the following content-clause ('that'). 'هرگز' = 'never'. 'مبادت' is an optative/negative-subjunctive formula meaning 'may it not be / may there never be' (classical negative jussive). 'بهی و مهی' are adjectival/substantive forms 'good/excellent and beautiful' (the two qualities said not to come into being); in poetry nouns/adjectives are often used substantively.
segments.s745-g127: 'به ایران' marks the location/field ('in/for Iran'); 'نخواهند' is the negated future/volitive 3rd-person plural 'they will not want/desire'. 'بیگانه‌ای' is indefinite 'a foreigner' (the -ای suffix makes it indefinite: 'a foreigner/an alien') and is the object of نخواهند.
segments.s745-g128: The correlative negation 'نه ... نه ...' = 'neither ... nor ...'. 'قیصر نژادی' = literally 'a Caesar by race/descent' (meaning an emperor of foreign lineage); 'فرزانه‌ای' = 'a sage/wise one' (indefinite). The speaker denies both types being wanted in Iran.
segments.s745-g97: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when' introducing the clause. آن نامه is the object 'that letter', and برخواند is a past, poetic verb meaning 'read aloud/recited' (verb before subject here, a common poetic order). مرد دبیر (literally 'the man, the scribe') is the subject placed after the verb.
segments.s745-g98: رخ 'face' is the grammatical subject of شد 'became' (simple past). نامور qualifies رخ as 'renowned', and the prepositional phrase به کردار قیر 'in the manner/appearance of tar' marks the resulting state; here کردار is used poetically as 'manner/appearance' and به introduces that resultant likeness.
segments.s745-g99: به دل گفت is an idiom meaning 'he said to his heart' or 'he thought to himself' (به + noun marking inward thought). کاین introduces the content of the thought ('that this...'), followed by بد کمین گراز 'a bad ambush of a boar' (noun phrase: adjective بد + head کمین + modifier گراز).
segments.s745-g100: دلیر here functions adverbially ('bravely'/'boldly') modifying the past first‑person verb آمدستم 'I came/approached' (آمدستم is an older poetic past form). به دامش is a prepositional object 'to/into his trap' (دام + 3rd‑person possessive -ش), and فراز 'up/on high' indicates direction or elevation accompanying the approach.
segments.s745-g101: This is a nominal phrase: شهنشاه و لشکر 'the shah and the army' followed by چو سیصد هزار, where چو before a numeral is used poetically to mean 'as many as' or 'about' (approximate number). The numeral phrase is placed after the nouns in typical poetic inversion.
segments.s745-g102: کس ... نداند شمار is a negative indefinite construction meaning 'no one knows/counts the number'. از پیل جنگش literally 'of/among his war‑elephants' specifies what number cannot be counted; نداند is the present/past-prose infinitive/poetic form 'knows' and شمار is 'number/count.'
segments.s745-g103: مرا ('me', written here split as م + را) is the accusative object 'me'. خواست is the past 'wanted' or 'wished', and افگند is the verb 'to cast/throw' (here part of the verb phrase 'خواست افگند' = 'he wished to throw/was about to throw'). در دام اوی is the prepositional phrase 'into his trap' (اوی = 'his/he').
segments.s745-g104: که introduces a clause giving the wished consequence or curse. تاریک 'dark' is predicative, بادا is the optative particle 'may it be' (used to express a wish or curse), and سرانجام اوی 'his end/fate' is the object of that wish—together 'may his end be dark.'
